class_schedule.topic: Why Educators Should Code: Computational Thinking and Beyond
learning_objectives: Define computational thinking and its four components as they apply to education
Explain why programming literacy matters for educational technologists
Set up a Python development environment and write a first program
readings: Wing, J. - Computational Thinking
Grover, S. & Pea, R. - Computational Thinking in K-12: A Review
Automate the Boring Stuff with Python, Ch. 0 (Introduction)

lecture_notes.introduction: You are not becoming a software engineer. You are becoming an educational technologist who can speak the language of technology, automate repetitive tasks, build prototypes, evaluate technical claims, and understand the systems you specify and procure. Programming literacy for educators means being able to read code, write simple scripts, and think computationally about problems. Computational thinking is not about coding per se; it is about problem-solving using decomposition, pattern recognition, abstraction, and algorithm design. These are the same skills you teach students, and programming is the medium through which you practice and demonstrate them. This course teaches Python because it reads like English, has a gentle learning curve, and powers the data analysis and automation tools used in modern educational technology.
key_concepts: Computational thinking: decomposition (break problems into parts), pattern recognition (find similarities), abstraction (identify the essential), algorithm design (create step-by-step solutions)
Why educators need programming: automate grading workflows, analyze student data, build learning tools, evaluate ed-tech products, communicate with developers
Python: readable syntax, versatile, widely used in education and data science, free and cross-platform
Development environment setup: VS Code editor, Python 3 interpreter, terminal basics (cd, ls, python)
Your first program: print('Hello, World!'), input() for user interaction, variables for storing data
The edit-run-debug cycle: write code, run it, read the error, fix it, run again; errors are normal and informative
Comments: # This explains why, not what; documentation for your future self and collaborators
The educator's programming mindset: good enough is good enough; you do not need to write perfect code to save hours of manual work
lecture_notes.demonstration: Install Python and VS Code, write a program that asks the user's name and grade level, prints a personalized welcome message with f-strings, and calculates how many school days remain in the year
setup_requirements: Computer with admin privileges for software installation

review.value: Problem-solving framework: decomposition (break it down), pattern recognition (find similarities), abstraction (identify the essential), algorithm design (create step-by-step solutions). Applies to lesson planning, curriculum design, and data analysis.

Reads like English (if score >= 90: grade = 'A'), gentle learning curve, rich libraries for data analysis and web development, most common language in data science and ed-tech tools, and completely free.
print('Hello') displays text. name = input('What is your name? ') captures user text into a variable. These two functions enable interactive programs from day one.
Python error messages tell you what went wrong and where. SyntaxError: you typed something Python does not understand. NameError: you used a variable that does not exist. Read the last line first.

class_schedule.topic: Variables, Data Types, and Basic Operations
learning_objectives: Declare and use variables of different data types for educational data
Perform arithmetic and string operations relevant to grade calculations
Write programs that process user input and produce formatted output
readings: Automate the Boring Stuff with Python Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Variables are containers for data. Understanding data types lets you work with numbers (test scores, attendance counts), text (student names, feedback comments), and boolean values (is_passing, has_iep). Today we build the foundation for all programming in this course by learning to store, manipulate, and display data. Every educational technology application you will ever evaluate or build operates on these same primitives: numbers, text, and true/false values. The grade calculator you build today is a real tool you could use to save time computing weighted averages, and it demonstrates every concept in the session: variables, types, arithmetic, string formatting, and user input.
key_concepts: Variables: naming conventions (snake_case), assignment (student_name = 'Alex'), reassignment (score = score + 5)
Data types: int (whole numbers like 95), float (decimals like 3.14), str (text like 'Alex'), bool (True/False)
Type conversion: int('95') converts string input to integer; str(95) converts number to string for display; float('3.7') for decimals
String operations: concatenation ('Hello ' + name), f-strings (f'Score: {score}'), slicing (name[0:3]), methods (.upper(), .strip(), .replace())
Arithmetic operators: + - * / for basic math, // for integer division, % for remainder, ** for exponents
Formatted output: f'{average:.1f}%' formats a float to one decimal place; f'{name:>20}' right-aligns in a 20-character field
User input: always returns a string; convert to int or float before math; handle invalid input gracefully
Assignment vs equality: = assigns a value, == tests equality; a common source of confusion for beginners
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a grade calculator that takes three quiz scores as input, calculates the weighted average (different weights per quiz), displays a formatted report card with student name, individual scores, weighted average, and letter grade
setup_requirements: Practice exercises file with educational scenarios

review.value: f'Hello, {name}! Your average is {average:.1f}%' embeds variables and formatting directly in strings. The :.1f formats a float to one decimal. Much cleaner than string concatenation.
input() always returns a string. score = int(input('Enter score: ')) converts to integer for math. If the user types 'abc', this crashes; we will learn to handle that with try/except.
7 / 2 = 3.5 (float division). 7 // 2 = 3 (integer division, rounds down). This matters when dividing students into groups or calculating page counts.
Use descriptive snake_case names: student_score, class_average, total_points. Avoid single letters (x, n) except in short loops. Clear names make code self-documenting.

learning_objectives: Write if/elif/else statements to implement grading and classification logic
Use comparison and logical operators to create complex decision criteria
Design programs that route students or data based on multiple conditions
readings: Automate the Boring Stuff Ch. 2 (Flow Control)

lecture_notes.introduction: Programs that make decisions are programs that do useful work. Conditionals let your code choose between different paths based on data, just like the branching scenarios you design for learners in adaptive courseware. An automated grading script uses conditionals to convert numeric scores to letter grades. A student alert system uses conditionals to flag students at risk of failing. A scheduling tool uses conditionals to assign students to sections based on preferences and capacity. Today we learn to implement all of these decision patterns in Python. The conditional statement is the most practically useful construct in this entire course because it enables automation of any rule-based decision process.
key_concepts: if/elif/else syntax: if condition: (action) elif other_condition: (action) else: (default action); Python uses indentation to define blocks
Comparison operators: == (equal), != (not equal), < > <= >= for numeric and string comparisons
Logical operators: and (both must be true), or (either can be true), not (inverts a condition)
Nested conditionals: if inside if for multi-level decisions; use sparingly and prefer elif chains for readability
Guard clauses: check for invalid input first and return early; reduces nesting and improves readability
Common patterns: grade classification (score ranges to letters), eligibility checking (multiple criteria), input validation (is the value in range?)
Boolean variables: is_passing = score >= 60; store decisions as named booleans for clarity
Ternary expression: grade = 'Pass' if score >= 60 else 'Fail' for simple two-way decisions in one line
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a grade classifier that takes a numerical score, validates the input (0-100 range), converts to letter grade using the standard scale with +/- modifiers, determines pass/fail status, and generates an appropriate feedback message for each grade level
setup_requirements: Practice problems with educational decision scenarios

review.value: Python uses indentation (4 spaces) to define code blocks. Code inside an if statement must be indented. This is not cosmetic; incorrect indentation causes IndentationError. This enforces readable code structure.

Always validate input before using it: if not 0 <= score <= 100: print('Invalid score'). For text input: try: score = int(input_text) except ValueError: print('Please enter a number'). Prevents crashes on bad data.
Use elif for mutually exclusive conditions (grade ranges). Use nested if when conditions are independent (check age AND check grade). elif chains are easier to read and less error-prone.
is_eligible = gpa >= 3.0 and credits >= 60 and not on_probation. Store complex conditions in named booleans. if is_eligible: is clearer than repeating the full condition.

learning_objectives: Write for and while loops to process lists of student data
Use lists to store and manipulate collections of educational records
Apply loop patterns to common educational data processing tasks
readings: Automate the Boring Stuff Ch. 2 (continued) and Ch. 4 (Lists)

lecture_notes.introduction: Loops let you process thousands of records with a few lines of code. For educators, this means automating tasks like calculating class averages from a roster of scores, identifying students missing assignments, generating personalized feedback for each student, and cleaning up data exports. A for loop says 'do this for each item in the collection,' and it is the single most time-saving construct in programming for educational technologists. Combined with lists (ordered collections of items), loops enable you to work with entire classrooms, grade books, and enrollment records programmatically. The class roster processor you build today replaces 30 minutes of spreadsheet work with a 2-second script.
key_concepts: Lists: ordered, mutable collections created with [item1, item2, ...]; store student names, scores, or any sequence of related data
For loops: for student in roster: processes each student identically; the most common loop pattern for educational data
While loops: while response != 'quit': continues until a condition changes; useful for interactive data entry and menu-driven programs
Range function: for i in range(10): generates numbers 0-9; range(1, 31) for days of the month; range(0, 101, 10) for grade buckets
List operations: append() adds items, len() counts items, sorted() orders items, sum() totals numeric lists
List indexing and slicing: roster[0] is the first student, roster[-1] is the last, roster[2:5] is a subset
List comprehensions: [s for s in scores if s >= 70] filters in one line; [s.upper() for s in names] transforms in one line
Accumulator pattern: total = 0; for score in scores: total += score; then average = total / len(scores)
Breaking and continuing: break exits the loop early; continue skips to the next iteration; use for special-case handling
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a class roster processor that reads a list of student names and scores, calculates the class average, identifies the highest and lowest scores, counts students scoring above 80%, generates a grade distribution histogram (text-based), and flags students below 60% for intervention
setup_requirements: Loop practice exercises with educational scenarios

review.value: Use for when you know how many iterations (process each student in a list). Use while when you do not know (keep asking for input until user types 'quit'). For loops are more common in data processing.
Concise list creation from loops: passing = [s for s in students if s['score'] >= 70]. Equivalent to a 4-line for loop but more Pythonic. Use for simple filtering and transformation.
total = 0; count = 0; for score in scores: total += score; count += 1; average = total / count. The fundamental pattern for computing aggregates over a collection.
Infinite while loop: forgot to update the condition variable. Off-by-one: range(10) gives 0-9, not 1-10. Modifying a list while iterating: causes skipped items. Iterate over a copy instead.

learning_objectives: Define and call functions to organize code into reusable, testable components
Use parameters, return values, and default arguments effectively
Refactor procedural code into well-organized functions
readings: Automate the Boring Stuff Ch. 3 (Functions)
Python documentation: Defining Functions

lecture_notes.introduction: Functions are the organizational unit of code. A function encapsulates a specific task so you can name it, reuse it, and test it independently. Without functions, your programs become long, repetitive scripts that are hard to debug and impossible to maintain. With functions, you build a personal library of useful tools: calculate_weighted_average(), generate_report_card(), flag_at_risk_students(). Each function does one thing well, accepts inputs through parameters, and returns a result. Today we learn to write functions that transform your single-use scripts into reusable tools. The function library you build in this course becomes a practical toolkit you can use throughout your career as an educational technologist.
key_concepts: Function definition: def calculate_average(scores): defines a function with a parameter; the body is indented below
Parameters and arguments: parameters are variable names in the definition; arguments are actual values passed when calling the function
Return values: return average sends a value back to the caller; functions without return implicitly return None
Default arguments: def generate_report(student, format='text'): makes format optional with a default value
Multiple return values: return average, letter_grade returns a tuple; avg, grade = calculate(scores) unpacks it
Docstrings: triple-quoted string at the top of a function describes its purpose, parameters, and return value
Scope: variables defined inside a function are local; this prevents naming conflicts between functions
Refactoring: extracting repeated code into a function; if you copy-paste code, it should probably be a function
Calling functions: result = calculate_average([85, 92, 78]) passes a list and captures the return value
lecture_notes.demonstration: Refactor the grade calculator from previous weeks into well-organized functions: validate_score(), calculate_weighted_average(), determine_letter_grade(), generate_feedback(), print_report_card(). Show how the same functions can process one student or a whole class.
setup_requirements: Grade calculator code from previous sessions

review.value: Reusability: write once, call many times. Readability: named functions communicate intent. Testability: test each function independently. Maintainability: fix a bug in one place, fixed everywhere.

def calculate_average(scores):
    '''Calculate the arithmetic mean of a list of numeric scores.
    Returns float average or None if the list is empty.'''
    Documents the function for anyone reading the code, including your future self.
return sends a value back for further use: avg = calculate_average(scores). print() displays text but returns None. Functions should return values; printing is for the final display layer.
Variables inside a function cannot accidentally affect variables outside it. student_name inside process_student() is separate from student_name in your main code. This prevents subtle bugs.

class_schedule.topic: Dictionaries and Structured Data for Education
learning_objectives: Use dictionaries to represent structured educational records
Build data models for students, courses, and assessments using nested dictionaries
Perform lookups, filtering, and aggregation on dictionary-based data
readings: Automate the Boring Stuff Ch. 5 (Dictionaries)
Python documentation: dict data type

lecture_notes.introduction: Dictionaries are the Python data structure that most closely matches how educational data is naturally organized. A student record has labeled fields: name, ID, grade level, scores, advisor. A course has a title, section, enrollment, and schedule. An assessment has questions, point values, and rubrics. Dictionaries store all of this with named keys instead of cryptic numeric indices. When you read data from a CSV file, an LMS export, or an API response, it typically becomes a list of dictionaries in Python. Mastering dictionaries means mastering the representation of educational data in code. Today we build structured data models for educational contexts and learn to query, filter, and aggregate data in these structures.
key_concepts: Dictionary creation: student = {'name': 'Alex', 'grade': 10, 'gpa': 3.5} stores labeled data as key-value pairs
Accessing values: student['name'] returns 'Alex'; student.get('email', 'N/A') returns a default if the key is missing
Modifying dictionaries: student['gpa'] = 3.6 updates a value; student['email'] = 'alex@school.edu' adds a new key
Iterating: for key, value in student.items(): processes all fields; for name in student.keys(): lists field names
Nested dictionaries: course = {'title': 'Python', 'students': [student1, student2]} for complex data models
List of dictionaries: roster = [{'name': 'Alex', 'score': 85}, {'name': 'Sam', 'score': 92}] represents a class roster
Filtering: [s for s in roster if s['score'] < 70] finds struggling students using list comprehension on dictionaries
Aggregation: sum(s['score'] for s in roster) / len(roster) computes class average from a list of student dictionaries
Dictionary as lookup table: grade_scale = {'A': (90, 100), 'B': (80, 89)} maps labels to ranges for quick reference
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a student information system as a list of dictionaries: each student has name, ID, grade level, course enrollments (list), assessment scores (dict), and calculated GPA. Implement functions to add students, update scores, calculate GPAs, find honor roll students, and generate class rank reports.
setup_requirements: Sample student data in dictionary format

review.value: Lists store items by position: scores[0]. Dictionaries store items by name: student['gpa']. Use dictionaries when data has labels (field names). Use lists when data is a sequence of similar items.
student.get('email', 'No email on file') returns the default if the key does not exist, instead of crashing with KeyError. Essential when processing real data where some records may be incomplete.
student['courses'][0]['grade'] accesses the grade for the first course. Real educational data is always nested: students have courses, courses have assignments, assignments have scores.
JSON files (used by LMS exports and APIs) map directly to Python dictionaries. json.load(file) converts JSON to a Python dict. If you understand dictionaries, you understand JSON data.

class_schedule.topic: Working with Data Files: CSV and JSON
learning_objectives: Read and write CSV files using Python for processing LMS grade exports
Parse JSON data files for course content and API configuration
Build data processing pipelines that transform educational data between formats
readings: Automate the Boring Stuff Ch. 9 (Reading and Writing Files)
Python csv and json module documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Educational data lives in files: grade exports from Canvas or Blackboard come as CSV files, course content configurations use JSON, survey results arrive in spreadsheet formats, and student information systems export data in various tabular formats. Being able to read, process, and write these files programmatically is the single most immediately useful programming skill for an educational technologist. A script that reads a Canvas grade export, calculates weighted final grades, identifies students below the passing threshold, and produces a formatted report saves hours every grading period. Today we learn the file I/O skills that make this automation possible, working with the exact file formats you encounter in educational technology practice.
key_concepts: Text file I/O: open('file.txt', 'r') for reading, 'w' for writing; with statement ensures proper file cleanup
CSV reading: csv.DictReader(file) reads each row as a dictionary keyed by header names; row['Student Name'] is self-documenting
CSV writing: csv.DictWriter(file, fieldnames=headers) writes dictionaries as CSV rows; produces clean spreadsheet-ready output
JSON reading: json.load(file) converts a JSON file to Python dictionaries and lists; instant access to structured data
JSON writing: json.dump(data, file, indent=2) writes Python data structures as formatted JSON files
Data cleaning during import: stripping whitespace, converting types, handling empty cells, standardizing formats
Error handling for files: FileNotFoundError when file does not exist, csv.Error for malformed CSV, json.JSONDecodeError for invalid JSON
Path handling: os.path.join() for platform-independent file paths; pathlib.Path for modern path manipulation
Real-world application: reading a Canvas gradebook CSV export and computing final grades automatically
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build an LMS grade processor that reads a Canvas-format CSV grade export, handles missing scores and excused assignments, calculates weighted final averages based on assignment group weights, determines letter grades, flags students below 70% for early alert, and writes a summary report to a new CSV file

review.value: csv.DictReader reads CSV rows as dictionaries: row['Final Score'] instead of row[12]. Self-documenting, resilient to column reordering, and the right choice for any CSV with headers.

JSON is the data format of web APIs and educational standards. LTI configurations, xAPI learning records, course catalog data, and most API responses use JSON. Reading JSON with json.load() is a core skill.
with open('grades.csv') as f: reader = csv.DictReader(f) guarantees the file is closed properly, even if an error occurs. Always use with instead of bare open().
Real data is messy: extra spaces, empty cells, inconsistent capitalization. Strip and normalize immediately: name = row['Name'].strip().title(); score = float(row['Score']) if row['Score'] else None.

class_schedule.topic: Data Analysis with pandas for Educators
learning_objectives: Use pandas DataFrames to analyze educational datasets efficiently
Perform grouping, aggregation, and filtering on student performance data
Create basic data visualizations from educational data
readings: Python for Data Analysis Ch. 5 (pandas basics)
pandas documentation: 10 Minutes to pandas

lecture_notes.introduction: pandas is the Python library that replaces Excel for data analysis. It provides the DataFrame, a table-like data structure that handles thousands of rows efficiently, automates complex calculations, and produces reproducible results. For educational technologists, pandas transforms grade analysis, enrollment reporting, assessment item analysis, and learning analytics from manual spreadsheet tasks into automated, repeatable workflows. After today, you will be able to load any educational CSV file into pandas and answer questions about the data in minutes instead of hours. This is the most practically valuable session in the course for your day-to-day work as an educational technologist.
key_concepts: DataFrame creation: df = pd.read_csv('grades.csv') loads a CSV file into a pandas DataFrame in one line
Viewing data: df.head(), df.shape, df.dtypes, df.describe() provide quick overviews of the dataset
Column selection: df['Score'] for one column, df[['Name', 'Score', 'Grade']] for multiple columns
Filtering rows: df[df['Score'] >= 90] returns only A-grade students; chainable with & (and) and | (or)
GroupBy: df.groupby('Section')['Score'].mean() computes average score per section in one line
Aggregation: df.groupby('Course').agg({'Score': ['mean', 'min', 'max'], 'Student': 'count'}) computes multiple stats
Missing data: df['Score'].isna().sum() counts missing scores; df['Score'].fillna(0) replaces with zero
Sorting: df.sort_values('GPA', ascending=False).head(10) finds top 10 students by GPA
Basic plotting: df['Score'].hist() draws a histogram; df.groupby('Section')['Score'].mean().plot.bar() draws a bar chart
Computed columns: df['Weighted'] = df['Score'] * df['Weight'] creates a new column from existing ones
lecture_notes.demonstration: Load a multi-section course gradebook into pandas, compute weighted final grades, compare section averages, identify the most and least successful assessment items, find students at risk of failing, and produce a visual summary with histograms and bar charts
setup_requirements: Jupyter notebook with pandas and matplotlib

review.value: A programmable spreadsheet: df = pd.read_csv('grades.csv') loads data; df[df['Score'] < 60] filters; df.groupby('Section')['Score'].mean() summarizes. Handles any size dataset reproducibly.
df.groupby('Section')['Score'].mean() groups rows by section and computes the average score for each. The single most useful pandas operation for educational data analysis.

pandas: reproducible (same script, same result), scalable (millions of rows), automatable (run monthly). Excel: manual (different clicks each time), limited (slows at 100K rows), fragile (formula errors compound).
df['Score'].hist(bins=10) shows the grade distribution. df.groupby('Assignment')['Score'].mean().plot.bar() compares assignments. One line of code for each chart.

learning_objectives: Use Python to make API requests to educational platforms and data sources
Automate repetitive file management and data processing tasks
Build scripts that collect data from web sources for educational research
readings: Automate the Boring Stuff Ch. 12 (Web Scraping)
Python requests library quickstart documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: The most practical programming skill for an educational technologist may be automation: writing scripts that do in seconds what would take you hours manually. API access lets you pull data from LMS platforms programmatically, and web scraping lets you collect data from public educational sources. Beyond web interaction, Python automation covers file management (renaming, organizing, converting), report generation (pulling data and formatting it), email automation, and data merging across systems. Today we combine API interaction with automation scripts to build tools that save real time in educational technology practice. The ROI on learning these skills is immediate: a 20-line script that runs in 2 seconds can replace a 30-minute manual workflow you repeat weekly.
key_concepts: HTTP basics: GET requests retrieve data, POST submits data; status codes (200=success, 404=not found) indicate outcomes
Python requests library: response = requests.get(url) makes a web request; response.json() parses JSON responses
API interaction: Canvas REST API for course data, grades, enrollments; authenticate with API tokens in headers
JSON response processing: parse API responses into Python dictionaries, extract needed fields, save to CSV
File automation: os.rename() for batch renaming, shutil.copy() for backups, glob.glob() for finding files by pattern
Report generation: combine data from multiple sources into a formatted output file (CSV, HTML, or text)
Scheduling: running scripts automatically; task scheduler (Windows) or cron (Mac/Linux) for periodic execution
Email automation: smtplib for sending automated notifications or reports to stakeholders
Ethical considerations: respect terms of service, rate limits, and data privacy when accessing external data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a Python automation suite with three tools: (1) a Canvas API client that pulls all student grades for a course and saves them as a local CSV, (2) a file organizer that sorts a downloads folder by file type, (3) a report generator that merges student data from multiple CSVs and produces a combined summary
setup_requirements: Canvas API documentation and test token (or mock API)
Multiple CSV files for merging exercise

review.value: With Python and the Canvas API, you can pull all student grades in one call, identify missing assignments, and generate customized reports. Tasks taking 30 minutes manually done in 2 seconds programmatically.

If you do a task more than three times, automate it. Rename 200 files? Script it. Generate monthly reports? Script it. Check for missing submissions? Script it. Educational technologists who script save hundreds of hours per year.
API tokens grant access to student data. Never hardcode tokens in scripts. Store in environment variables: token = os.environ['CANVAS_TOKEN']. Never commit tokens to git repositories.
import glob, os; for f in glob.glob('*.pdf'): os.rename(f, f'processed_{f}') renames all PDF files. Combine with shutil.move() to organize files into folders automatically.

class_schedule.topic: Building Educational Web Applications with Flask
learning_objectives: Build a simple web application using Python Flask framework
Create web forms that collect and process educational data
Understand the request-response cycle of web applications
readings: Flask Quickstart documentation
HTML forms basics (MDN Web Docs)

lecture_notes.introduction: Web applications are the delivery mechanism for most educational technology. Understanding how web apps work, even at a basic level, transforms how you evaluate, specify, and communicate about ed-tech products. Flask is a lightweight Python web framework that lets you build functional web applications with minimal code. You are not becoming a web developer, but being able to build a simple prototype demonstrates a concept to stakeholders, lets you test an idea before investing in development, and gives you the vocabulary to communicate effectively with developers who build the products you specify and evaluate.
key_concepts: Flask basics: app = Flask(__name__); @app.route('/') def home(): return 'Hello'; creates a web application in 4 lines
Routes: @app.route('/quiz') maps a URL to a Python function; the function returns the page content
Templates: render_template('quiz.html', questions=questions) generates HTML pages with dynamic data using Jinja2
Forms: HTML <form> elements collect user input; Flask accesses form data with request.form['answer']
GET vs POST: GET requests retrieve pages, POST requests submit data; forms use POST for data submission
Session data: session['score'] persists data between page loads for multi-page interactions like quizzes
JSON responses: return jsonify({'score': 85, 'grade': 'B'}) for API-style responses that JavaScript can consume
Development server: app.run(debug=True) runs a local server for testing; debug mode shows errors and auto-reloads
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a simple quiz web application with Flask: a home page listing available quizzes, a quiz page that presents questions one at a time, a results page showing the score with feedback, and an admin page showing all quiz results in a table
setup_requirements: Python with Flask installed (pip install flask)
HTML template files for the quiz pages
Sample quiz question data in JSON format

review.value: @app.route('/quiz/<int:quiz_id>') def show_quiz(quiz_id): connects a URL pattern to a Python function. When someone visits /quiz/3, Flask calls show_quiz(3). This is how web apps map URLs to content.
render_template('results.html', score=85, total=100) passes Python data to an HTML template. The template uses {{ score }} to display the value. Separates logic (Python) from presentation (HTML).

A working Flask prototype communicates your idea more effectively than a document. Stakeholders can interact with it. Developers can see exactly what you want. A 50-line prototype can replace a 10-page specification.

Route, endpoint, template, form submission, session, API response, HTTP method. Understanding these terms enables productive conversations with developers about the educational tools you are specifying.

class_schedule.topic: Data Visualization and Reporting for Education
learning_objectives: Create professional data visualizations for educational reporting using matplotlib and seaborn
Select appropriate chart types for different educational data questions
Generate automated visual reports suitable for administrators and faculty
readings: Storytelling with Data by Cole Nussbaumer Knaflic, Ch. 1-4
matplotlib and seaborn documentation (gallery examples)

lecture_notes.introduction: Data visualization is how educational technologists communicate findings to decision-makers: principals, deans, provosts, and school board members. A well-designed chart communicates a semester of student performance data in seconds. A poorly designed chart obscures the message or misleads the audience. Python's matplotlib and seaborn libraries create every chart type used in educational reporting: bar charts for comparing sections, line charts for tracking trends, scatter plots for correlating variables, and box plots for showing grade distributions. Today we learn to create charts that are accurate, clear, and actionable, following data visualization best practices that serve educational communication rather than just technical display.
key_concepts: Chart selection: bar chart for category comparison (section averages), line chart for trends (enrollment over years), scatter plot for relationships (study hours vs grades)
matplotlib basics: fig, ax = plt.subplots(); ax.bar(sections, averages); ax.set_xlabel('Section'); ax.set_title('Average Scores by Section')
Seaborn for statistical visualization: sns.boxplot(data=df, x='Section', y='Score') shows distributions; sns.heatmap() for correlation matrices
Grade distribution histogram: ax.hist(scores, bins=[0,60,70,80,90,100]) with custom bins matching grade thresholds
Multi-panel dashboards: fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2) for presenting multiple views of the same dataset
Annotation and emphasis: highlighting specific data points, adding reference lines, using color to draw attention
Accessibility: colorblind-friendly palettes, sufficient font size, descriptive titles, labeled axes
Saving and sharing: fig.savefig('report.png', dpi=150) for documents; fig.savefig('report.pdf') for print quality
Automated report generation: functions that produce standard visualizations from any class dataset
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build an end-of-semester course performance dashboard: (1) grade distribution histogram with pass/fail threshold line, (2) assignment score trends over the semester, (3) section comparison bar chart, (4) correlation scatter plot between homework and exam scores. Save as a multi-panel PNG for inclusion in a faculty report.
setup_requirements: Jupyter notebook with matplotlib and seaborn
Course gradebook dataset from previous sessions

review.value: Comparing groups: bar chart. Showing trends: line chart. Showing relationships: scatter plot. Showing distributions: histogram or box plot. Start with the question, then choose the chart.
Every chart should answer a specific question. Title the chart with the finding, not the data type. 'Section B scores 12% lower than Section A' is better than 'Score Comparison Across Sections'.
Remove anything that does not inform: 3D effects, decorative graphics, excessive gridlines. Use color purposefully (highlight the finding, not the decoration). Simple charts communicate faster.
Write a function: def generate_course_report(csv_path, output_path). Run it each semester with different data. Same professional report, zero manual chart-building time.

class_schedule.topic: Assessment Item Analysis and Learning Analytics
learning_objectives: Perform classical test theory item analysis on assessment data using Python
Calculate difficulty and discrimination indices for assessment items
Interpret item analysis results to improve assessment quality
readings: Assessment in Education: Principles, Policy & Practice (item analysis chapter)
Classical Test Theory overview (educational measurement text)

lecture_notes.introduction: Assessment item analysis is one of the most valuable applications of programming for educational technologists. Item analysis evaluates each question on a test: How difficult was it? Does it discriminate between students who know the material and those who do not? Are distractors working properly? Manual item analysis is tedious and rarely done despite its importance. With Python, you can analyze hundreds of items across thousands of student responses in seconds, producing reports that identify poorly performing questions, highlight items needing revision, and improve assessment validity over time. This is a direct application of programming skills to improve educational quality.
key_concepts: Item difficulty: p = correct_responses / total_responses; values from 0 (nobody got it) to 1 (everybody got it); ideal range 0.3-0.7
Item discrimination: comparing performance on each item between high-scoring and low-scoring students; high discrimination means the item distinguishes well
Point-biserial correlation: correlation between item score (0/1) and total test score; rpb > 0.3 is good discrimination
Distractor analysis: for multiple-choice, count how many students chose each option; good distractors attract low-performing students
Reliability: Cronbach's alpha measures internal consistency; alpha > 0.7 generally acceptable for classroom assessments
Item-total correlation: if removing an item increases alpha, the item may be hurting test reliability
Automated analysis pipeline: read item responses from CSV, compute all statistics, flag problematic items, generate report
Visualization: difficulty-discrimination scatter plot identifies items in each quality quadrant (easy/hard x discriminating/non-discriminating)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build an assessment item analysis tool that: reads a CSV of student responses to a 30-question multiple-choice exam, computes difficulty and discrimination for each item, performs distractor analysis, calculates Cronbach's alpha, flags items for review (too easy, too hard, negative discrimination), and produces a visual item analysis report
setup_requirements: Jupyter notebook with pandas, numpy, matplotlib
Sample assessment response data (CSV: student x item matrix)

review.value: p = 0.85 means 85% of students got it right; too easy to discriminate. p = 0.15 means only 15% got it right; may be too hard or poorly written. Target 0.3-0.7 for maximum information.

Correlates item score with total score. Positive rpb: students who scored well overall tend to get this item right (good). Negative rpb: high-performing students are getting it wrong (red flag: ambiguous question or wrong answer key).
For MCQ with options A, B, C, D (C correct): good distractors attract low-performing students evenly across A, B, D. If nobody picks B, it is obviously wrong and should be revised.
Running item analysis after each exam and revising flagged items improves test quality over semesters. Python makes this practical to do routinely instead of never.

class_schedule.topic: Working with LMS APIs: Canvas, Blackboard, and Google Classroom
learning_objectives: Interact with Learning Management System APIs to extract and manipulate educational data
Build automated workflows for common LMS administrative tasks
Understand LTI and xAPI standards for educational tool integration
readings: Canvas REST API documentation (getting started)
LTI 1.3 specification overview
xAPI (Experience API) specification overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Learning Management Systems are the central platforms of educational technology, and their APIs unlock powerful automation capabilities. The Canvas REST API, for example, provides programmatic access to courses, assignments, grades, enrollments, discussions, and analytics. Google Classroom and Blackboard offer similar APIs. As an educational technologist, the ability to write Python scripts that interact with your institution's LMS lets you automate grade exports, generate custom reports the LMS does not provide, monitor student engagement patterns, and build custom integrations between systems. Today we learn to work with LMS APIs, focusing on Canvas as the primary example but with patterns that apply to any educational platform API.
key_concepts: Canvas API structure: base URL + endpoint (e.g., /api/v1/courses/123/enrollments) returns JSON data; paginated for large results
Authentication: API access tokens generated in Canvas settings; passed as Authorization: Bearer token header
Common endpoints: /courses (list courses), /courses/123/students (enrollment), /courses/123/assignments (assignments), /courses/123/analytics
Pagination: Canvas returns pages of results; follow the Link header rel='next' URL to get subsequent pages
Grade export automation: pull all grades for a course, compute custom weighted averages, generate reports not available in Canvas UI
Enrollment management: programmatic enrollment updates, section management, cross-listing automation
LTI (Learning Tools Interoperability): standard for integrating external tools into LMS platforms; understand the launch flow
xAPI (Experience API): standard for recording learning experiences from any source; statements follow Actor-Verb-Object pattern
Data privacy: API access to student data is governed by FERPA; handle student records with appropriate care
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a Canvas API automation toolkit: (1) authenticate and list the instructor's courses, (2) pull all student submissions for a specific assignment, (3) identify students who have not submitted, (4) generate a 'missing work' email draft for each student, (5) export a custom grade report combining data from multiple assignments
setup_requirements: Canvas test instance or mock API server

review.value: Generate token in Canvas Settings > Approved Integrations. Use in requests: headers = {'Authorization': f'Bearer {os.environ["CANVAS_TOKEN"]}'}. Never share tokens or commit them to code repositories.

Canvas returns 10-100 results per page. Check response.links['next']['url'] for the next page URL. Loop until no 'next' link exists. Without pagination handling, you only get the first page of results.

LTI allows external tools to launch from within the LMS with authenticated context (who the user is, what course they are in). Understanding LTI helps you evaluate and integrate third-party educational tools.
API access to student data is covered by FERPA. Only access data you need for your role. Store student data securely. Do not share API tokens. Log access for auditability.

learning_objectives: Integrate all course skills into a complete, functional educational tool
Apply software design principles to educational technology development
Package and document a tool for use by non-technical educational colleagues
readings: Software Design for Flexibility, selected sections
User documentation best practices for non-technical audiences

lecture_notes.introduction: This session brings together everything you have learned to build a complete educational tool. Not a toy example but a real tool that solves a real problem for real educators. The tool must: read data from a file or API, process it with the logic you have learned (conditionals, loops, functions, pandas), produce useful output (reports, visualizations, or transformed data), handle errors gracefully, and be documented clearly enough that a colleague who does not program can use it. This is the engineering session where your individual skills combine into a coherent, usable product. The tool you build today becomes the foundation for your final project and a portfolio piece demonstrating your capabilities.
key_concepts: Tool architecture: separate data loading, processing, analysis, and output into distinct functions; each does one thing well
Configuration: separate settings (file paths, thresholds, weights) from code; use a config file or command-line arguments
Error handling: graceful failure with informative messages; the user should never see a Python traceback
Input validation: check that files exist, data is in expected format, required columns are present, before processing
Output formatting: produce output that non-programmers can use directly: CSV for spreadsheets, formatted text for reports, PNG for presentations
User documentation: write instructions assuming the reader has never used Python; include setup steps, example usage, and expected output
Testing: verify your tool produces correct results with known data; document the test cases
Iterative development: start with the minimum useful version, verify it works, then add features one at a time
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a complete Student Success Early Alert Tool: reads enrollment and grade data, computes risk scores based on configurable criteria (missing assignments, below-threshold grades, attendance), produces a prioritized alert list with intervention recommendations, and generates a visual risk dashboard. Walk through the architecture, error handling, and documentation.
setup_requirements: Python environment with all course libraries

review.value: load_data() handles file I/O. calculate_risk_scores() handles the analysis logic. generate_report() handles the output. Each function can be tested, debugged, and modified independently.
Thresholds (passing_score = 60), file paths, and weights should be in a config file, not in the code. When parameters change, the user edits config.json instead of modifying Python code.
Instead of crashing with FileNotFoundError, display: 'Error: Could not find grades.csv. Please ensure the file exists in the data/ directory.' The user knows what went wrong and how to fix it.
Step 1: Install Python (link to installer). Step 2: Open Terminal. Step 3: Type 'python alert_tool.py grades.csv'. Step 4: Open report.csv in Excel. Assume zero technical knowledge.

learning_objectives: Present a complete educational technology tool demonstrating all course skills
Evaluate peer projects and provide constructive feedback
Articulate the value of programming skills in educational technology practice
readings: Course materials review for final assessment

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session celebrates your transformation from non-programmer to educational technologist who codes. You present the tools you have built, demonstrating how programming skills enhance educational technology practice. Your projects show real tools solving real problems: grade analysis automation, assessment item analysis, student success early alerts, enrollment reporting, LMS data integration, and learning analytics dashboards. Each presentation tells the story of a problem you identified, a solution you built, and the time and insight your tool provides. These projects become portfolio pieces that demonstrate a skill set increasingly valued in educational technology: the ability to build, not just evaluate, data-driven educational solutions.
key_concepts: Project presentation: problem identification, solution design, implementation walkthrough, demonstration, results, and reflection
Peer evaluation: constructive feedback on functionality, usability, documentation, and practical value
Portfolio development: your final project plus course exercises demonstrate programming capability for job applications
Continuing learning: Python for education resources, ISTE standards for computational thinking, online courses for deeper skills
Career applications: ed-tech evaluation, learning analytics, assessment design, LMS administration, instructional design, data-informed decision making
The programming-augmented educator: you are not a programmer; you are an educator who programs; the domain expertise is the differentiator
Skill inventory: variables, conditionals, loops, functions, dictionaries, file I/O, CSV/JSON, pandas, visualization, APIs, web apps, automation
Next steps: Python data science track, SQL for database queries, R for statistical analysis, JavaScript for web interactivity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Instructor demonstrates the evaluation rubric with a sample project, showing how practical value, technical implementation, documentation quality, and presentation clarity are assessed

review.value: Your final project demonstrates: Python proficiency, educational domain knowledge, data analysis skills, tool design ability, and professional documentation. Keep it accessible for interviews and job applications.

You do not need to write production software. You need to automate your own workflows, analyze your own data, prototype your own ideas, and communicate technically with developers. That is what this course delivered.
A 20-line grade processing script that runs in 2 seconds replaces 30 minutes of manual Excel work. Run weekly, that is 25 hours saved per semester. Programming literacy pays for itself immediately.
Variables, conditionals, loops, functions, dictionaries, file I/O (CSV, JSON), pandas, visualization, web APIs, Flask web apps, LMS integration, item analysis, automation, tool packaging.

questions.question: Write a Python function called classify_score that takes a numerical score (0-100) as input and returns the letter grade: A (90-100), B (80-89), C (70-79), D (60-69), F (below 60). Include input validation that returns 'Invalid' for scores outside 0-100.

rubric.key: Correct conditional logic with proper thresholds
Correct function definition and return values

questions.question: What is the difference between = and == in Python? Give an example of when using the wrong one would cause a bug in a grade processing program.
questions.answer: = is the assignment operator (sets a variable to a value): grade = 'A'. == is the comparison operator (tests if two values are equal): if grade == 'A'. Bug example: in a grade processing program, writing 'if student_grade = "A":' instead of 'if student_grade == "A":' would cause a SyntaxError in Python. Even more dangerously, in some contexts confusing the two could silently assign a value instead of checking it, making every student appear to have the same grade.

rubric.key: Provides a specific educational bug example
Explains the consequence of the error

questions.question: Write Python code that asks the user for a temperature in Fahrenheit, converts it to Celsius using the formula C = (F - 32) * 5/9, and prints whether school will be cancelled (below 0C), delayed (0-10C), or normal schedule (above 10C).

rubric.key: Correct conditional logic with proper thresholds

questions.question: Write Python code that takes a list of student scores [85, 72, 90, 68, 95, 78, 82, 61, 88, 74] and prints: (1) the class average, (2) the number of students scoring 80 or above, (3) the highest and lowest scores, and (4) a list of scores below 70.
questions.answer:
scores = [85, 72, 90, 68, 95, 78, 82, 61, 88, 74]
average = sum(scores) / len(scores)
above_80 = len([s for s in scores if s >= 80])
highest = max(scores)
lowest = min(scores)
below_70 = [s for s in scores if s < 70]
print(f'Average: {average:.1f}')
print(f'Students 80+: {above_80}')
print(f'Highest: {highest}, Lowest: {lowest}')
print(f'Below 70: {below_70}')

questions.question: Write a Python function called calculate_weighted_average that takes a dictionary of assignment scores and a dictionary of assignment weights, and returns the weighted average. Example: scores = {'Quiz 1': 85, 'Midterm': 78, 'Final': 92}, weights = {'Quiz 1': 0.2, 'Midterm': 0.3, 'Final': 0.5}.

rubric.key: Handles edge case of zero weights

questions.question: Why is Python better than Excel for processing 500 student records from an LMS export? Give three specific advantages and describe the general approach for reading and processing the CSV file.
questions.answer: (1) Reproducibility: a Python script produces identical results every time. Excel depends on manual steps that may vary. (2) Automation: the script can run automatically whenever new data is exported, saving recurring manual effort. (3) Scalability: the same script works for 500 or 50,000 records without modification; Excel slows down with large datasets. Approach: use csv.DictReader to read the file, loop through each row to extract student name and scores, calculate derived values (weighted average, missing assignments, risk flags), and write results to a new CSV file.

rubric.key: Three distinct valid advantages of Python over Excel
Correct general approach for CSV processing
Mentions practical benefits like automation or error handling

quizzes.topic: File Processing, pandas, and Data Analysis

questions.question: Write Python code using pandas to load a CSV file called 'grades.csv', compute the average score per assignment (columns 'Quiz 1', 'Quiz 2', 'Midterm', 'Final'), identify the assignment with the lowest average, and create a bar chart comparing assignment averages.
questions.answer:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.read_csv('grades.csv')
assignments = ['Quiz 1', 'Quiz 2', 'Midterm', 'Final']
averages = df[assignments].mean()
lowest = averages.idxmin()
print(f'Lowest average: {lowest} ({averages[lowest]:.1f})')
averages.plot.bar(title='Average Scores by Assignment')
plt.ylabel('Average Score')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('assignment_averages.png')

rubric.key: Correct pandas CSV reading and column selection
Bar chart with labels and title

questions.question: You have a CSV file with columns: Student, Section, Score. Write pandas code to produce a summary showing the count, mean, min, and max score for each section, sorted by mean score descending.
questions.answer:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv')
summary = df.groupby('Section')['Score'].agg(['count', 'mean', 'min', 'max'])
summary = summary.sort_values('mean', ascending=False)
print(summary.round(1))

rubric.key: Correct groupby with multiple aggregation functions

questions.question: A colleague gives you a CSV with student records. Some cells contain 'N/A', some scores are negative, and some names have extra whitespace. Describe three data quality issues and write the pandas code to fix each one.
questions.answer: (1) 'N/A' strings: df = pd.read_csv('file.csv', na_values=['N/A']) converts them to NaN on import. (2) Negative scores: df.loc[df['Score'] < 0, 'Score'] = None replaces impossible values with NaN. (3) Whitespace in names: df['Name'] = df['Name'].str.strip() removes leading/trailing spaces. Each fix should be logged: print(f'Fixed {count} issues') for auditability.

rubric.key: Three distinct data quality issues identified
Correct pandas code for each fix
Mentions logging or auditing the fixes

questions.question: Write Python code using the requests library to make a GET request to a Canvas API endpoint (https://canvas.example.com/api/v1/courses/123/students) with an API token from an environment variable. Parse the JSON response and extract a list of student names.
questions.answer:
import os
import requests

token = os.environ.get('CANVAS_TOKEN')
url = 'https://canvas.example.com/api/v1/courses/123/students'
headers = {'Authorization': f'Bearer {token}'}
response = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
response.raise_for_status()
students = response.json()
names = [s['name'] for s in students]
print(names)

rubric.key: Correct API request with authentication header
Correct JSON parsing and name extraction

questions.question: Explain what a Flask route is and write a simple Flask application with two routes: (1) a home page at '/' that displays 'Welcome to the Quiz App', and (2) a quiz page at '/quiz' that displays a list of 3 questions passed from Python to the template.

rubric.key: Correct Flask app creation and route definitions
Clear explanation of what a route is

questions.question: You need to create a visualization showing grade distributions for 4 course sections. Which chart type would you use and why? Describe the matplotlib/seaborn code structure (pseudocode is fine) for creating a 2x2 multi-panel figure with one panel per section.
questions.answer: Box plot or histogram for each section. Box plot is preferred because it shows median, quartiles, and outliers in a compact format, making cross-section comparison easy. Code structure: fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(10, 8)). For each section (enumerate through sections list), plot to axes[row][col]: axes[0][0].hist(section_a_scores, bins=10). Set title, xlabel, ylabel for each panel. fig.suptitle('Grade Distributions by Section'). plt.tight_layout(). plt.savefig('distributions.png'). Alternatively, a single box plot: sns.boxplot(data=df, x='Section', y='Score') compares all sections in one panel.

rubric.key: Appropriate chart type selection with justification

quizzes.topic: Item Analysis, LMS Integration, and Comprehensive Review

questions.question: An assessment item has difficulty p = 0.95 and point-biserial discrimination rpb = 0.05. Interpret these values and recommend whether to keep, revise, or remove the item. What might cause these values?
questions.answer: Interpretation: p = 0.95 means 95% of students answered correctly; the item is very easy. rpb = 0.05 means the item barely discriminates between high and low performers; both groups get it right. Recommendation: revise or remove. An item this easy provides almost no information about student knowledge differences. Possible causes: (1) the concept is genuinely easy and well-taught (consider removing from the test since it does not differentiate), (2) the correct answer is obvious from the question wording or elimination of distractors, (3) the item appeared on a practice test and students memorized the answer. Revision approach: increase complexity, improve distractors to be more plausible, or test a deeper understanding of the same concept.

questions.question: Write a Python function that reads a CSV of student quiz responses (columns: Student, Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4, Q5 with values 0 or 1), calculates the difficulty (p-value) for each question, and returns a dictionary mapping question names to their difficulty values.
questions.answer:
import csv

def calculate_difficulty(csv_path):
    with open(csv_path) as f:
        reader = csv.DictReader(f)
        rows = list(reader)
    questions = ['Q1', 'Q2', 'Q3', 'Q4', 'Q5']
    difficulty = {}
    for q in questions:
        correct = sum(1 for row in rows if int(row[q]) == 1)
        difficulty[q] = correct / len(rows)
    return difficulty

rubric.key: Returns dictionary mapping questions to p-values
Handles the data correctly (string to int conversion)

questions.question: Describe the complete architecture of an educational data tool you would build to help advisors identify at-risk students. Include: data sources, processing steps, output format, and how you would make it usable by a non-technical advisor.
questions.answer: Data sources: (1) LMS gradebook CSV export with assignment scores and submission dates, (2) attendance records from student information system, (3) optionally Canvas API for real-time data. Processing: (1) Load and merge data by student ID, (2) calculate risk indicators: missing assignments count, current grade below threshold, declining grade trend over past 4 weeks, attendance below 80%, (3) compute a composite risk score weighting each indicator, (4) categorize as High/Medium/Low risk. Output: CSV file with columns: Student Name, ID, Risk Level, Current Grade, Missing Assignments, Risk Factors. Also generate a summary chart showing risk distribution. For non-technical users: package as a command-line tool with one-step execution ('python risk_report.py grades.csv attendance.csv'), write a one-page instruction guide with screenshots, and offer to set up automated weekly execution.

rubric.key: Clear processing steps with specific risk indicators

major_assignments.title: Educational Data Processing Tool
major_assignments.description: Build a Python tool that solves a real data processing problem for educators. The tool must: read data from a CSV or JSON file (e.g., LMS grade export, survey results, enrollment data), process the data using functions, conditionals, and loops, produce useful output (summary report, cleaned data file, or formatted analysis), include error handling for common data quality issues, and be documented for a non-technical audience. Submit working code, sample data, output, and documentation.
objectives: Apply Python programming skills to a genuine educational data problem
Build a functional tool that automates a manual process saving real time

Produce user documentation accessible to a non-technical educator

Variable names and comments make code self-documenting
Functional tool solving a real educational data processing problem

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are mandatory. Programming skills are built through practice with instructor support. More than 2 unexcused absences will reduce the final grade by 5% per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late coding assignments lose 5% per day for up to 5 days. Code must run without errors to receive credit. Extensions granted for documented emergencies.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussing approaches and debugging is encouraged. Sharing code is not permitted. If you get help from a classmate or online resource, cite it in your comments.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be your own. Using AI coding assistants is permitted for learning but all generated code must be understood; you must be able to explain every line. Submitting unmodified AI-generated code is prohibited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Computer with Python 3.x and VS Code installed. Internet access for library installation and API exercises. All software is free.
